<type>discovery</type>
<title>Existing Balanced-Body Token Handler Patterns for Self-Eval Implementation Reference</title>
<subtitle>Eight balanced-body handlers show consistent pattern: singleton check, pipe-delimited parsing, length validation, silent truncation over rejection, return empty string on success</subtitle>
<facts>
<fact>SKILL_USED handler uses seenSkillNames Set for deduplication (first reason wins), returns empty string on duplicate to strip token without adding to array</fact>
<fact>GOAL_DONE, RESTART, SKILL_PROPOSE, WIKI_QUERY, SPECIALIST_PROXY, MOA_PLAN handlers all use singleton pattern: if already captured (not null), return null to leave duplicate token visible</fact>
<fact>Length validation strategy shifted 2026-05-03 from hard rejection to silent truncation: reason.length > BALANCED_REASON_MAX triggers .slice(0, BALANCED_REASON_MAX), preventing infinite continuation loop bug</fact>
<fact>Pipe-delimited tokens (SKILL_USED, SKILL_PROPOSE, WIKI_QUERY, SPECIALIST_PROXY) validate both parts: first part matches charset regex, second part checks length bounds</fact>
<fact>GOAL_DONE and RESTART reject pipe character in reason field (reason.includes("|")) to prevent format confusion</fact>
<fact>MOA_PLAN has minimal JSON-like validation (startsWith("{") && endsWith("}")) leaving full parsing to consumer</fact>
<fact>Command handlers registered in src/index.ts: bot.command("skillusage", handleSkillUsage) at line 720, bot.command("status", handleStatus) at line 729, bot.command("session", handleSession) at line 726</fact>
</facts>
<narrative>The workflow examined the actual implementation of existing balanced-body token handlers to extract the concrete coding patterns that a new SELF_EVAL token should follow. All eight handlers demonstrate a consistent architecture: (1) singleton or deduplication check at the top (if already captured, return null for singleton pattern or empty string for dedup pattern), (2) pipe-delimited field extraction with .indexOf("|") and .slice(), (3) charset validation via regex test for identifiers, (4) length bounds checking with the critical 2026-05-03 pattern change - instead of rejecting over-length content (which caused visible token remnants and infinite continuation loops), handlers now silently truncate with .slice(0, BALANCED_REASON_MAX), and (5) return empty string on success to strip the token from visible text. The GOAL_DONE handler explicitly rejects pipe characters (reason.includes("|")) to prevent format ambiguity since it's a single-field token. The command handler registration shows /skillusage exists as a pattern for displaying observability data, which relates to the Adversarial phase's "orphan data" critique - a belowThreshold flag would be meaningless unless consumed somewhere like /status or /skillusage. This implementation reference provides the concrete template for adding <<SELF_EVAL:score|evidence>> with proper validation: singleton check (selfEval !== null), pipe-split, score parseInt with 0-100 bounds check, evidence length cap (likely 500 to match BALANCED_REASON_MAX), and silent truncation strategy rather than rejection.</narrative>

<observation>
<type>discovery</type>
<title>Observability Command Handler Pattern for Self-Eval Display Integration</title>
<subtitle>handleSkillUsage shows pattern for reading store, formatting entries, and displaying tracked metrics via Telegram command interface</subtitle>
<facts>
<fact>handleSkillUsage command (line 529+ in src/commands/misc.ts) demonstrates observability pattern: detect skill directory, call usageStore.migrateFromDisk() to sync state, read usageStore.listAll() for display data</fact>
<fact>Command performs graceful degradation: SKILL_DIR not set shows warning message, zero entries shows informative "no SKILL.md files" message, read errors show failure reason</fact>
<fact>usageStore tracks sweep results (added/newlyOrphaned/deOrphaned arrays) from migrateFromDisk operation before displaying current state</fact>
<fact>Entries filtered by pinned flag suggesting multi-tiered display (pinned skills likely shown separately from others)</fact>
<fact>Total count calculated from Object.entries(all.skills).map() pattern, suggesting selfEval store would follow similar { [key: string]: Entry } structure</fact>
</facts>
<narrative>The workflow investigated how the bridge displays tracked observability metrics to understand where self-eval scores would be consumed, directly addressing the Adversarial phase's "orphan data" critique of the Simplicity proposal. The /skillusage command implementation reveals the established pattern: (1) gracefully handle preconditions (skill directory detection with warning if unavailable), (2) perform synchronization/migration step (usageStore.migrateFromDisk) before reading, (3) call store's listAll() method to get complete dataset, (4) transform entries via Object.entries().map() for formatting, (5) filter/segment data for multi-tiered display (pinned vs unpinned), and (6) send formatted output via ctx.reply(). This pattern provides the concrete template for making self-eval scores actionable rather than orphaned: create a selfEvalStore with listAll() method returning scores by session/task, add display logic to /status command (which already shows session metrics) or create dedicated /selfeval command, format output showing recent scores with belowThreshold flags highlighted, and include summary statistics (average score, below-threshold count). The use of migrateFromDisk suggests stores support lazy initialization and state synchronization, meaning selfEvalStore could follow the same append-only pattern with periodic aggregation rather than requiring eager index maintenance.</narrative>
